section.title: The Fool Says There Is No God
section.sectionRationale: A wisdom lament diagnosing the universal condition of humanity apart from God, later quoted at length by Paul in Romans 3 as scriptural proof that none are righteous. It closes Book I's cluster of laments with a longing look toward the salvation of Israel, and stands as one of the Bible's clearest short statements of total human corruption and hoped for restoration.
section.thesis: Apart from God, humanity's corruption is universal and total, yet even here the psalm does not end in despair but reaches for the salvation that will come from Zion.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the fool's denial and the universal corruption it reveals; the oppression of God's people by workers of iniquity; and the longing cry for salvation from Zion.

units.label: There is no one who does good
units.note: the fool's denial and universal corruption

verses.text:
The {{nabal|fool}} says in his heart, “There is no God.” 
They are corrupt; their acts are vile. There is no one who does good.
The LORD looks down from heaven upon the sons of men to see if any understand, if any seek God.
All have turned away, they have together become corrupt; there is no one who does good, not even one.

units.label: They devour my people like bread

verses.text:
Will the workers of iniquity never learn? 
They devour my people like bread; they refuse to call upon the LORD.
There they are, overwhelmed with dread, for God is in the company of the righteous.
You sinners frustrate the plans of the oppressed, yet the LORD is their {{machaseh|shelter}}.

units.label: Let Jacob rejoice
units.note: the longing for salvation from Zion

verses.text:
Oh, that the {{yeshuah|salvation}} of Israel would come from Zion! 
When the LORD restores His captive people, let Jacob rejoice, let Israel be glad!

nabal.gloss: Fool, one morally and spiritually senseless, not merely intellectually deficient. In Hebrew thought the fool's denial of God is a matter of the heart and will, a practical atheism lived out in corrupt action rather than a reasoned philosophical position.

machaseh.gloss: Shelter, refuge, a place of protection from storm or attack. Even while sinners frustrate the plans of the oppressed, the psalm insists the LORD Himself remains their machaseh, their true and sufficient hiding place.

yeshuah.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue. The psalmist longs for this yeshuah to come out of Zion for all Israel, the same word later given as the personal name of Jesus, the one through whom that salvation finally comes.

basic.context: The psalm opens with the fool's quiet, practical denial, 'There is no God', spoken in his heart, and moves at once to describe the corruption that follows from it. God looks down and finds no one who does good, not even one. That corruption then turns predatory, oppressing God's people, who have nowhere else to turn but to the LORD as their shelter. The psalm ends with a longing prayer that salvation would come from Zion and restore God's people to joy.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not a psalm about a few obviously wicked people out there; verses 2-3 describe the human race as a whole, apart from God's grace, which is exactly how Paul later uses it in Romans 3 to prove that every person, Jew and Gentile alike, stands guilty before God. The psalm both diagnoses the disease and points, in its final verse, toward the only cure.
basic.originalLanguages: The nabal, fool, denies God not as an intellectual argument but as a practical stance of the heart, lived out in corrupt action. Even under oppression the LORD remains machaseh, shelter, for the righteous, and the psalm's closing hope is that yeshuah, salvation, the very word behind the name Jesus, would come from Zion.
basic.typology: Zion, the place from which salvation is longed for here, points forward to the greater Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to Christ Himself, who brings the salvation this psalm can only long for and name, but not yet see.
basic.christ: Paul quotes this psalm in Romans 3:10-12 as part of his case that all have sinned and no one is righteous, precisely so that he can then present Christ as the only remedy, the righteousness of God given through faith in Jesus to all who believe, apart from human merit.
basic.god: God is shown searching, looking down from heaven to see if anyone understands or seeks Him, revealing both His patience and the depth of the problem He finds. Yet He is also shelter for the oppressed and the one from whom true salvation will come.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:10-12 quotes this psalm directly as scriptural proof of universal sin. Psalm 53 repeats this psalm nearly word for word with small variations. Isaiah 59:2 shows the same theme, that sin separates humanity from God.
basic.oneStory: This psalm names humanity's universal condition apart from grace and then reaches, in its very last breath, for the salvation that must come from outside that condition, from Zion, a longing finally answered when salvation came from Zion in the person of Jesus Christ.

context.literary: A wisdom psalm shaped as a lament, moving from diagnosis, verses 1-3, to the specific oppression of the righteous, verses 4-6, and closing with a prayer of longing, verse 7; it shares an almost identical text with Psalm 53, likely composed for a different liturgical setting.
context.historical: Composed during a period when the faithful in Israel experienced real oppression from those who denied God in practice, if not always in explicit confession, making the closing hope for restoration from captivity especially poignant.
context.canonical: One of the Bible's foundational texts on universal human sinfulness, quoted at length by Paul in Romans 3 as part of the case for humanity's need of a Savior who fulfills the psalm's closing hope of salvation from Zion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the practical atheism and resulting corruption of humanity as a whole, to comfort the oppressed with the assurance that God is their shelter, and to voice hope for the salvation and restoration of God's people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a universal condition rather than prescribing a specific ritual or law; its enduring, prescriptive force is theological, that all people apart from grace are corrupt, and that hope rests in God's promised salvation, not human merit.

debates.question: Does 'there is no one who does good, not even one' allow for any exceptions among the righteous mentioned later in the psalm?

views.label: The statement is a rhetorical, sweeping indictment of humanity's condition apart from grace, and the 'righteous' of verse 5 are those made righteous by trust in God, not by inherent goodness
views.case: Paul applies this very language in Romans 3 to the entire human race including believers prior to faith, while still speaking elsewhere of the righteous as those declared righteous through faith, not those who never sinned.

views.label: The psalm's 'fool' and 'righteous' describe two distinct groups already present in Israel, the openly wicked versus the faithful covenant community
views.case: Verses 4-6 clearly distinguish 'my people' and 'the righteous' from the oppressing fools, suggesting the psalm's harshest language targets a specific corrupt group rather than every individual without distinction.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the fool's corruption is real and serious; the New Testament's use in Romans 3 shows the church has long read the psalm's universal language as establishing the doctrine that righteousness before God comes only through faith, never through inherent human goodness.

study.originalLanguages: The nabal's denial of God is a practical, heart level stance rather than a reasoned argument, and it results in corruption so total that God, looking down from heaven, finds no one who does good. Yet machaseh, shelter, remains available to the oppressed in the LORD Himself, and the psalm's closing hope rests in yeshuah, salvation, out of Zion.

typology.type: The longed for salvation coming out of Zion, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: The salvation of God accomplished through Jesus Christ, who came from the line of David associated with Zion and brought the restoration this psalm could only anticipate.

typology.type: God's search for anyone who understands or seeks Him, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: Answered fully only in Christ, the one truly righteous man, through whom sinners are now counted righteous by faith.

study.christ: Paul takes this psalm's stark verdict, 'there is no one who does good, not even one', as scriptural proof in Romans 3:10-12 that every person stands condemned before God apart from grace. He then presents Christ as the answer this psalm longs for but cannot itself provide: the righteousness of God given through faith in Jesus to all who believe, the salvation that finally comes from Zion in a person rather than merely a hope.
study.god: God is shown as the searching judge who looks down from heaven and finds universal corruption, yet He does not abandon the oppressed; He remains their shelter, and He is Himself the source from whom the longed for salvation will come.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes this psalm directly as part of his argument that all have sinned and no one is righteous before God.
A near duplicate of this psalm, likely composed or preserved for a different liturgical occasion, underscoring its importance.
Declares that iniquities have separated the people from God, echoing this psalm's diagnosis of universal sin.

study.oneStory: This psalm diagnoses the human condition apart from grace as total corruption, yet it closes by reaching for a salvation that must come from outside that condition, from Zion. That salvation arrived in Jesus Christ, who fulfills the psalm's closing hope and answers Paul's use of its opening verdict with the good news of righteousness by faith.

quiz.q: What does the fool's denial of God in verse 1 primarily describe?

options.text: A practical, heart level rejection of God lived out in corrupt action, not merely an intellectual argument.
options.note: The nabal denies God in his heart and then acts corruptly, showing this is a matter of the will as much as the mind.

options.text: A carefully reasoned philosophical case against God's existence.
options.note: The psalm presents the denial as a heart posture that produces corrupt action, not a formal philosophical argument.

options.text: A momentary doubt quickly resolved by prayer.
options.note: The psalm describes settled corruption, not a fleeting doubt.

options.text: A rare condition limited to only a few individuals.
options.note: Verses 2-3 broaden this to describe humanity as a whole apart from grace, not merely a rare few.

quiz.q: How does Paul use this psalm in Romans 3?

options.text: He quotes it as scriptural proof that all people, apart from grace, are corrupt and none is righteous.
options.note: Romans 3:10-12 draws directly on this psalm's language to build the case for universal sinfulness.

options.text: He cites it to prove that only Gentiles are sinful.
options.note: Paul's argument in Romans 3 explicitly includes Jew and Gentile alike, not Gentiles only.

options.text: He uses it to argue that good works alone can restore a person to righteousness.
options.note: Paul's point is the opposite, that righteousness comes by faith, not works, precisely because none does good on their own.

options.text: He references it only in passing with no theological weight.
options.note: This understates its role; Paul builds a central part of his argument on this psalm's verdict.

quiz.q: What is the LORD described as for the oppressed in verse 6?

options.text: Their shelter, machaseh, even while sinners frustrate their plans.
options.note: The psalm insists God remains a true refuge for the righteous despite ongoing oppression.

options.text: Their judge who condemns them alongside the wicked.
options.note: The oppressed are distinguished from the wicked and are shown God's protection, not condemnation.

options.text: A distant observer with no active care for their situation.
options.note: The text actively names God as their shelter, not a distant or uninvolved figure.

options.text: Their eventual replacement by human rescuers.
options.note: This misses the direct point that God Himself, not a human substitute, is named as their shelter.

quiz.q: What does the psalm long for in its final verse?

options.text: That salvation would come from Zion and God would restore His captive people to rejoicing.
options.note: Verse 7 closes with this longing hope for restoration and joy.

options.text: That the fools would simply be ignored rather than judged.
options.note: The psalm's hope is restorative and salvific, not merely an appeal to ignore wrongdoing.

options.text: That Israel would gain military dominance over surrounding nations.
options.note: The text speaks of salvation and restoration from captivity, not a call to military conquest.

options.text: That the psalmist alone would be personally rescued from his enemies.
options.note: The hope in verse 7 is corporate, for Israel and Jacob as a whole, not merely for the psalmist individually.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's closing hope connect to Christ?

options.text: The salvation longed for from Zion is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, who brings the righteousness and restoration this psalm could only anticipate.
options.note: Paul's use of the psalm's diagnosis in Romans 3 sets up exactly this need, met fully in Christ's righteousness given by faith.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Jerusalem's future rebuilding.
options.note: The psalm expresses a theological and spiritual longing, not a specific date for rebuilding.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament's teaching.
options.note: Paul's direct quotation of this psalm in Romans 3 shows a strong, explicit New Testament connection.

options.text: It refers only to a political restoration with no spiritual significance.
options.note: This understates the psalm's deeper spiritual longing for salvation, which the New Testament reads as fulfilled in Christ.

questions: The fool's denial of God happens quietly, in his heart, before it shows up in corrupt action. Where might practical unbelief, living as though God does not see or matter, be shaping your own choices?

Paul uses this psalm to say none of us is righteous on our own. How does it change your posture toward God and others when you accept this diagnosis honestly rather than comparing yourself favorably to 'worse' sinners?
The oppressed in this psalm have the LORD as their shelter even while their plans are frustrated. Where do you need to trust God as your refuge even though your circumstances have not yet improved?
The psalm ends with longing rather than resolution, 'oh, that salvation would come'. What are you still longing for God to complete or restore in your own life or community?

Salvation finally came from Zion in the person of Jesus. How does knowing that this psalm's hope has already been answered in Christ shape the way you pray for the needs and injustices you still see around you?

translationNotes.note: Fool renders nabal, describing moral and spiritual senselessness rather than mere intellectual deficiency; the denial is a heart posture, not primarily a philosophical claim.
This psalm is nearly identical to Psalm 53, with small differences in wording and in the divine name used, likely reflecting use in two different liturgical settings.
Salvation renders yeshuah, the same root later given as the personal name Jesus, linking this psalm's closing hope to its ultimate New Testament fulfillment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 14:1-7. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file.
